practices.title: Never hardcode API keys or secrets
practices.explanation: Hardcoded secrets in Swift files are visible in the IPA binary. Use Info.plist with build-time substitution or a keychain.

practices.title: Never store sensitive data in UserDefaults
practices.explanation: UserDefaults is stored unencrypted in a plist file. Use Keychain Services for tokens, passwords, and keys.

practices.title: Validate server certificates — never disable ATS
practices.good:
// Use default ATS settings; pin certificates for sensitive endpoints
let session = URLSession(configuration: .default, delegate: pinningDelegate, delegateQueue: nil)
practices.explanation: Disabling ATS allows unencrypted HTTP and opens the app to man-in-the-middle attacks. Use certificate pinning for sensitive APIs.

practices.title: Sanitize user input before displaying in WebViews
practices.good:
let escaped = userInput.replacingOccurrences(of: "<", with: "<") webView.loadHTMLString("

\(escaped)

", baseURL: nil)
practices.explanation: Injecting unsanitized input into WebViews enables cross-site scripting. Escape HTML entities or use WKWebView content rules.

practices.title: Use protocols to define interfaces for testability
practices.explanation: Protocol-based dependencies enable mock injection in tests and reduce coupling between components.

practices.title: Use extensions to organise conformances
practices.explanation: Extensions group protocol conformances separately from core logic, improving readability and navigation.

practices.title: Use MARK comments to section large files
practices.explanation: MARK comments create navigable sections in Xcode's jump bar, making large files easier to browse.

practices.title: Keep view controllers under 300 lines
practices.bad: // 500-line ViewController with networking, UI, and data logic
practices.good: // Split into ViewModel, Coordinator, and lean ViewController
practices.explanation: Massive view controllers are the most common iOS anti-pattern. Use MVVM or similar to separate concerns.

practices.title: Use Result type for error handling instead of optional returns
practices.explanation: Optional return types lose error information. Use Result or throws to propagate error context.

practices.title: Avoid force unwrap (!) — use guard let or if let
practices.good: guard let name = user.name else { return }
practices.explanation: Force unwrap crashes at runtime if the value is nil. Use optional binding for safe access.

practices.title: Use [weak self] in closures to prevent retain cycles
practices.explanation: Strong self references in closures create retain cycles that leak memory. Use [weak self] for closures stored by other objects.

practices.title: Handle async/await errors with do-catch
practices.explanation: try! crashes on errors. Always use do-catch for async operations that can fail.

practices.title: Avoid retain cycles — use weak references for delegates
practices.explanation: Strong delegate references create retain cycles between parent and child objects, leaking memory. Always use weak.

practices.title: Use lazy properties for expensive initialisations
practices.explanation: Eager initialization runs even if the property is never used. lazy defers creation until first access.

practices.title: Perform heavy work on background queues
practices.explanation: Heavy computation on the main thread freezes the UI. Use GCD or async/await with background actors.

practices.title: Avoid creating objects in cellForRowAt — reuse and cache
practices.explanation: Creating objects per cell causes excessive allocation during scrolling. Cache formatters and heavy objects as properties.
